paragraphs.text:
%md

# Assignment 2

## MovieLens Data Analysis using Apache Spark and Cassandra

Name : Yang Huan

Matric Number: P161796

msg.data:
Assignment 2

MovieLens Data Analysis using Apache Spark and Cassandra

Name : Yang Huan

Matric Number: P161796

paragraphs.text:
%md

# 1.0 Introduction

The MovieLens 100K dataset is a widely used dataset that contains information about users, movies and movie ratings. In this assignment, Apache Spark is used to process the dataset while Cassandra is used to store and retrieve the processed data.

The purpose of this assignment is to demonstrate the use of Spark RDDs, DataFrames and Cassandra integration in a big data environment.

msg.data:
1.0 Introduction

The MovieLens 100K dataset is a widely used dataset that contains information about users, movies and movie ratings. In this assignment, Apache Spark is used to process the dataset while Cassandra is used to store and retrieve the processed data.

The purpose of this assignment is to demonstrate the use of Spark RDDs, DataFrames and Cassandra integration in a big data environment.

paragraphs.text:
%md

# 1.1 Objectives

The objectives of this assignment are:

1. To load MovieLens data from HDFS.
2. To create Spark RDDs and DataFrames.
3. To perform data cleaning and preprocessing.
4. To store processed data into Cassandra.
5. To retrieve data from Cassandra.
6. To perform analytical queries using Spark SQL.
7. To interpret and discuss the analytical results.

msg.data:
1.1 Objectives

The objectives of this assignment are:

  1. To load MovieLens data from HDFS.
  2. To create Spark RDDs and DataFrames.
  3. To perform data cleaning and preprocessing.
  4. To store processed data into Cassandra.
  5. To retrieve data from Cassandra.
  6. To perform analytical queries using Spark SQL.
  7. To interpret and discuss the analytical results.

%md

### 2.0 Software Environment

- Apache Spark version: 2.3.0.2.6.5.0-292
- Cassandra version: 3.11.19
- Notebook environment: Apache Zeppelin
- Data storage: HDFS and Cassandra

## 3.0 Load Dataset from HDFS

The MovieLens 100K dataset was uploaded into HDFS before running the analysis. In this section, the three required files are loaded into Spark as RDDs.

The files used are:

- u.user: user information
- u.data: movie rating records
- u.item: movie information

msg.data:
3.0 Load Dataset from HDFS

The MovieLens 100K dataset was uploaded into HDFS before running the analysis. In this section, the three required files are loaded into Spark as RDDs.

The files used are:

  • u.user: user information
  • u.data: movie rating records
  • u.item: movie information

paragraphs.text:
%spark
val usersRDD = sc.textFile("hdfs:///user/maria_dev/ml-100k/u.user")
val ratingsRDD = sc.textFile("hdfs:///user/maria_dev/ml-100k/u.data")
val moviesRDD = sc.textFile("hdfs:///user/maria_dev/ml-100k/u.item")

println("Users Records: " + usersRDD.count())
println("Ratings Records: " + ratingsRDD.count())
println("Movies Records: " + moviesRDD.count())

println("Sample User:")
println(usersRDD.first())

println("Sample Rating:")
println(ratingsRDD.first())

println("Sample Movie:")
println(moviesRDD.first())

# 4.0 Create RDD Objects

Spark RDDs (Resilient Distributed Datasets) are created from the MovieLens files. RDDs provide a distributed collection of records that can be processed in parallel across the Spark cluster.

msg.data:
4.0 Create RDD Objects

Spark RDDs (Resilient Distributed Datasets) are created from the MovieLens files. RDDs provide a distributed collection of records that can be processed in parallel across the Spark cluster.

paragraphs.text:
%spark

println("Users RDD Partitions: " + usersRDD.getNumPartitions)
println("Ratings RDD Partitions: " + ratingsRDD.getNumPartitions)
println("Movies RDD Partitions: " + moviesRDD.getNumPartitions)

println("\nFirst 3 Users:")
usersRDD.take(3).foreach(println)

### Discussion

The results show that all three RDDs were created successfully. Each dataset was divided into two partitions by Spark, which allows the data to be processed in parallel.

The first three records from the users dataset were displayed to verify that the data was loaded correctly. The output confirms that the RDDs are ready to be transformed into DataFrames for further analysis.

%md

# 5.0 Transform RDDs into DataFrames

Although RDDs are useful for distributed processing, DataFrames provide a more structured way to manage data and support Spark SQL operations. Therefore, the MovieLens RDDs are converted into DataFrames.

msg.data:
5.0 Transform RDDs into DataFrames

Although RDDs are useful for distributed processing, DataFrames provide a more structured way to manage data and support Spark SQL operations. Therefore, the MovieLens RDDs are converted into DataFrames.

paragraphs.text:
%md
# 5.1 Users DataFrame

msg.data: 5.1 Users DataFrame

paragraphs.text:
%spark

import spark.implicits._

val usersDF = usersRDD.map(_.split("\\|"))
  .map(x => (
    x(0).toInt,
    x(1).toInt,
    x(2),
    x(3),
    x(4)
  ))
  .toDF(
    "user_id",
    "age",
    "gender",
    "occupation",
    "zipcode"
  )

usersDF.show(5)

# 5.2 Ratings DataFrame

msg.data: 5.2 Ratings DataFrame

paragraphs.text:
%spark

val ratingsDF = ratingsRDD.map(_.split("\\t"))
  .map(x => (
    x(0).toInt,
    x(1).toInt,
    x(2).toInt,
    x(3).toLong
  ))
  .toDF(
    "user_id",
    "movie_id",
    "rating",
    "rating_timestamp"

ratingsDF.show(5)

%md

## 5.3 Movies DataFrame

The u.item file contains movie information and genre indicators. In this section, movie ID, movie title, release date and genre columns are extracted and converted into a Spark DataFrame.

msg.data:
5.3 Movies DataFrame

The u.item file contains movie information and genre indicators. In this section, movie ID, movie title, release date and genre columns are extracted and converted into a Spark DataFrame.

paragraphs.text:
%spark

import spark.implicits._

val genreNames = Array(
  "unknown", "Action", "Adventure", "Animation", "Children",
  "Comedy", "Crime", "Documentary", "Drama", "Fantasy",
  "Film-Noir", "Horror", "Musical", "Mystery", "Romance",
  "Sci-Fi", "Thriller", "War", "Western"
)

val moviesDF = moviesRDD.map(_.split("\\|"))
  .map(x => {
    val movieId = x(0).toInt
    val title = x(1)
    val releaseDate = x(2)

    val genres = genreNames.zip(x.slice(5, 24))
      .filter(g => g._2 == "1")
      .map(g => g._1)
      .mkString(",")

    (movieId, title, releaseDate, genres)
  })
  .toDF("movie_id", "title", "release_date", "genres")

moviesDF.show(5, false)
println("Movies Records: " + moviesDF.count())

|movie_id|title            |release_date|genres                   |
+--------+-----------------+------------+-------------------------+
|1       |Toy Story (1995) |01-Jan-1995 |Animation,Children,Comedy|
|2       |GoldenEye (1995) |01-Jan-1995 |Action,Adventure,Thriller|

paragraphs.text:
%md

### Discussion

The users, ratings and movies RDDs were successfully transformed into Spark DataFrames. Compared with RDDs, DataFrames provide a structured format with named columns, making the data easier to query and analyze using Spark SQL.

The displayed records confirm that the schema was correctly assigned for all three datasets. The data is ready for cleaning, Cassandra storage and analytical processing.

The displayed records confirm that the schema was correctly assigned for all three datasets. The data is ready for cleaning, Cassandra storage and analytical processing.

Before storing the data into Cassandra, basic preprocessing is performed to verify the quality of the MovieLens dataset.

6.0 Data Cleaning and Preprocessing

Before storing the data into Cassandra, basic preprocessing is performed to verify the quality of the MovieLens dataset.

usersDF.select("age")
  .describe()
  .show()

%spark

ratingsDF.select("rating")
  .describe()
  .show()

%md
### Discussion

The preprocessing results show that all three DataFrames contain the expected number of records. The users dataset contains 943 records, the ratings dataset contains 100,000 records, and the movies dataset contains 1,682 records.

The age values range from 7 to 73 years old, which appears reasonable for the MovieLens user population. In addition, movie ratings range from 1 to 5, matching the original MovieLens rating scale.

Based on these observations, the datasets are suitable for storage in Cassandra and further analytical processing.

msg.data:
Discussion

The preprocessing results show that all three DataFrames contain the expected number of records. The users dataset contains 943 records, the ratings dataset contains 100,000 records, and the movies dataset contains 1,682 records.

The age values range from 7 to 73 years old, which appears reasonable for the MovieLens user population. In addition, movie ratings range from 1 to 5, matching the original MovieLens rating scale.

Based on these observations, the datasets are suitable for storage in Cassandra and further analytical processing.

# 7.0 Store DataFrames into Cassandra

In this section, the processed Spark DataFrames are written into Cassandra tables. This step is required because Cassandra is used as the storage layer for the MovieLens data.

msg.data:
7.0 Store DataFrames into Cassandra

In this section, the processed Spark DataFrames are written into Cassandra tables. This step is required because Cassandra is used as the storage layer for the MovieLens data.

paragraphs.text:
%md
## 7.1 Configure Cassandra Connection

msg.data: 7.1 Configure Cassandra Connection

paragraphs.text:
%spark

spark.conf.set("spark.cassandra.connection.host", "127.0.0.1")

println("Cassandra connection host has been configured.")

msg.data: Cassandra connection host has been configured.

paragraphs.text:
%md
## 7.2 Write Users DataFrame into Cassandra

msg.data: 7.2 Write Users DataFrame into Cassandra

paragraphs.text:
%spark

usersDF.write
  .format("org.apache.spark.sql.cassandra")
  .options(Map(
    "table" -> "users",
    "keyspace" -> "movielens"
  ))
  .mode("append")
  .save()

println("Users DataFrame has been written into Cassandra.")

msg.data: Users DataFrame has been written into Cassandra.

%md

## 9.0.1 Rating Distribution Visualization

This visualization shows the distribution of rating scores in the MovieLens dataset.

  .groupBy("rating")
  .count()
  .orderBy("rating")

z.show(ratingDistribution)

### Discussion

The rating distribution shows how users rated movies in the dataset. Most ratings are between 3 and 5, which suggests that users generally gave moderate to positive ratings. Lower ratings such as 1 and 2 appear less frequently compared with higher ratings.

Discussion

The rating distribution shows how users rated movies in the dataset. Most ratings are between 3 and 5, which suggests that users generally gave moderate to positive ratings. Lower ratings such as 1 and 2 appear less frequently compared with higher ratings.

paragraphs.text:
%md
## 9.1 Average Rating for Each Movie

This analysis calculates the average rating received by each movie based on all user ratings.

msg.data:
9.1 Average Rating for Each Movie

This analysis calculates the average rating received by each movie based on all user ratings.

paragraphs.text:
%spark

ratingsFromCassandra.createOrReplaceTempView("ratings")
moviesFromCassandra.createOrReplaceTempView("movies")

val avgMovieRatings = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    m.movie_id,
    m.title,
    ROUND(AVG(r.rating),2) AS average_rating,
    COUNT(*) AS total_ratings
FROM ratings r
JOIN movies m
ON r.movie_id = m.movie_id
GROUP BY m.movie_id, m.title
ORDER BY m.movie_id
""")

avgMovieRatings.show(10, false)

%md

### Discussion

This table shows the average rating and total number of ratings for each movie. The results are ordered by movie ID to provide a general overview of the dataset rather than only showing the highest-rated movies.

msg.data:
Discussion

This table shows the average rating and total number of ratings for each movie. The results are ordered by movie ID to provide a general overview of the dataset rather than only showing the highest-rated movies.

paragraphs.text:
%md

## 9.2 Top Ten Movies with Highest Average Ratings

This analysis identifies the ten movies with the highest average ratings in the MovieLens dataset.

msg.data:
9.2 Top Ten Movies with Highest Average Ratings

This analysis identifies the ten movies with the highest average ratings in the MovieLens dataset.

paragraphs.text:
%spark

val top10Movies = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    m.title,
    ROUND(AVG(r.rating),2) AS average_rating,
    COUNT(*) AS total_ratings
FROM ratings r
JOIN movies m
ON r.movie_id = m.movie_id
GROUP BY m.title
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 50
ORDER BY average_rating DESC, total_ratings DESC
LIMIT 10
""")

top10Movies.show(false)

%md

### Discussion

Movies with only a few ratings may have very high average scores, but the result may not be reliable. Therefore, this analysis only considers movies with at least 50 ratings.

The result shows that movies such as Close Shave, Schindler's List and The Wrong Trousers received high average ratings from a reasonable number of users. This provides a more meaningful ranking of highly rated movies in the MovieLens dataset.

msg.data:
Discussion

Movies with only a few ratings may have very high average scores, but the result may not be reliable. Therefore, this analysis only considers movies with at least 50 ratings.

The result shows that movies such as Close Shave, Schindler’s List and The Wrong Trousers received high average ratings from a reasonable number of users. This provides a more meaningful ranking of highly rated movies in the MovieLens dataset.

paragraphs.text:
%md

## 9.3 Users Who Rated at Least 50 Movies and Their Favourite Genre

This analysis identifies users who have rated at least 50 movies and determines their favourite movie genre based on the genres they rated most frequently.

msg.data:
9.3 Users Who Rated at Least 50 Movies and Their Favourite Genre

This analysis identifies users who have rated at least 50 movies and determines their favourite movie genre based on the genres they rated most frequently.

paragraphs.text:
%spark

import org.apache.spark.sql.functions._

val activeUsers = ratingsFromCassandra
  .groupBy("user_id")
  .count()
  .filter($"count" >= 50)
  .select("user_id")

val ratingsWithGenres = ratingsFromCassandra
  .join(activeUsers, "user_id")
  .join(moviesFromCassandra, "movie_id")

val genreCounts = ratingsWithGenres
  .withColumn("genre", explode(split($"genres", ",")))
  .groupBy("user_id", "genre")
  .count()

val favouriteGenres = genreCounts
  .groupBy("user_id")
  .agg(max(struct($"count", $"genre")).as("fav"))
  .select(
    $"user_id",
    $"fav.genre".as("favourite_genre"),
    $"fav.count".as("genre_count")
  )
  .orderBy("user_id")

favouriteGenres.show(20, false)

println("Active Users: " + favouriteGenres.count())

msg.data:
import org.apache.spark.sql.functions._
activeUsers: org.apache.spark.sql.DataFrame = [user_id: int]
ratingsWithGenres: org.apache.spark.sql.DataFrame = [movie_id: int, user_id: int ... 5 more fields]
genreCounts: org.apache.spark.sql.DataFrame = [user_id: int, genre: string ... 1 more field]
favouriteGenres: org.apache.spark.sql.Dataset[org.apache.spark.sql.Row] = [user_id: int, favourite_genre: string ... 1 more field]
+-------+---------------+-----------+
|user_id|favourite_genre|genre_count|
+-------+---------------+-----------+
|1      |Drama          |107        |
|2      |Drama          |35         |
|3      |Drama          |22         |
|5      |Comedy         |82         |
|6      |Drama          |104        |
|7      |Drama          |146        |

### 9.3 Discussion

Users who rated at least 50 movies were identified as active users. The favourite genre of each active user was determined by counting how frequently each genre appeared in the movies they rated. This analysis helps reveal user preferences and viewing behaviour within the MovieLens dataset.

%spark

val youngUsers = usersFromCassandra
  .filter($"age" < 20)
  .select(
    "user_id",
    "age",
    "gender",
    "occupation"
  )

youngUsers.show(20, false)

println("Total Users Below 20: " + youngUsers.count())

paragraphs.text:
%md

# 10.0 Conclusion

This assignment demonstrated the use of Apache Spark and Cassandra for big data processing and analysis.

The MovieLens 100K dataset was successfully loaded from HDFS and transformed into Spark RDDs and DataFrames. Data cleaning and validation were performed before storing the processed data into Cassandra tables. The stored data was then retrieved from Cassandra to verify successful integration between Spark and Cassandra.

Several analytical tasks were completed, including calculating movie average ratings, identifying the top-rated movies, determining favourite genres of active users, identifying users below 20 years old, and finding scientists aged between 30 and 40 years old.

The results show that Spark provides efficient distributed data processing capabilities, while Cassandra offers scalable NoSQL storage for large datasets. Together, these technologies provide an effective platform for big data analytics.

msg.data:
10.0 Conclusion

This assignment demonstrated the use of Apache Spark and Cassandra for big data processing and analysis.

The MovieLens 100K dataset was successfully loaded from HDFS and transformed into Spark RDDs and DataFrames. Data cleaning and validation were performed before storing the processed data into Cassandra tables. The stored data was then retrieved from Cassandra to verify successful integration between Spark and Cassandra.

Several analytical tasks were completed, including calculating movie average ratings, identifying the top-rated movies, determining favourite genres of active users, identifying users below 20 years old, and finding scientists aged between 30 and 40 years old.

The results show that Spark provides efficient distributed data processing capabilities, while Cassandra offers scalable NoSQL storage for large datasets. Together, these technologies provide an effective platform for big data analytics.
